Introduction  
The Sky2 cutscene system is a method for designers and other content-creators to write a cutscene  
narrative (think of it as a script in the cinematic sense, not the TFBTool sense) in a fast, modular fashion.  
This allows for a lot of ﬂexibility in how we write cutscenes but keeps it all in a sane container, preventing  
any game-ruining breakages while leaving room for neat features like instant cutscene skipping and  
anything else we may want to add down the road.  
Unlike cutscenes in Sky1, when you write cutscene scripts, you are not actually making anything happen -  
merely writing the narrative that the cutscene's actors/cameras/etc. will follow. Think of it as being a  
1 of 14  
01/11/2016 03:29 PM  
Cutscene System - tfbwiki  
http://tfb-wiki/w/index.php/Cutscene_System  
screenwriter, then handing the instructions oﬀ to a director/producer to make it happen later, rather than  
being on the set from start to ﬁnish, making things happen one at a time.  
Though there is a lot of detail in how you can write cutscenes, actors' cutscene actions will generally be  
written with canned, modular pieces that let you say things like 'move to this waypoint,' 'play this  
animation,' or 'speak this dialogue.' The idea is to make the common things as accessible and easy as  
possible while leaving room for anything you can imagine.  
If you are looking to create a simple panning cutscene (e.g. show key, show locked door), check out the  
simpliﬁed cutscene system.  
Basic Components of a Cutscene  
A cutscene narrative is comprised of a few simple building blocks which have certain relationships: a  
cutscene, scenes, camera shots, objects, and their sequences. The narrative describes a timeline in which  
certain events are triggered, and most of the actual timing of the cutscene is controlled by cameras and  
sequences:  
The cutscene, which contains any number of scenes and objects  
Objects are the actors in your cutscenes  
Scenes are logical start/end points in your cutscene which contain any number of camera shots and  
scene objects  
Camera shots are instructions for positioning and transitioning between cameras  
Scene objects are the per-scene version of objects and are used for getting them to do things  
with a sequence  
Each sequence contains any number of steps which control the behavior of the object  
Steps are the smallest atom of a cutscene, and are simple behavioral operations like  
speaking dialogue, moving somewhere, or playing an animation.  
Cutscene Setup  
Setting up a cutscene to run involves a few straightforward steps, though  
what the cutscene does is very much up to you! The only requisite steps are as  
follows:  
Create a new macro specialization of the Cutscene - Deﬁne Cutscene  
Macro macro  
Use the Cutscene - Deﬁne Cutscene macro  
Add scenes and objects as needed (more on that, later)  
To play the cutscene, use the Cutscene - Play Cutscene macro and  
specify the macro specialization that you created as the *play cutscene  
macro parameter  
Playing a cutscene  
To check if the cutscene is done, check if the value, Cutscene State (in [global.lvl]), is 0.  
2 of 14

Cutscene System - tfbwiki  
http://tfb-wiki/w/index.php/Cutscene_System  
Beyond that, the only requirement is that the cutscene is scripted according to certain rules.  
Simple Cutscene  
To whet your appetite and to give you an idea of what a ﬁnished cutscene looks like, below is a very simple,  
but functional, cutscene that deﬁnes one object and contains two scenes (note: there's been some  
Photoshopping on this image to slim it down and reduce clutter - the macros used actually have more  
complex interfaces):

Cutscene Components In Depth  
In a general sense, all of the cutscene components are highly contextual: that is, if you nest (place one  
macro within the indentation of another) an Add Scene macro in the Deﬁne Cutscene macro, that means  
"add this scene to this cutscene". Along similar lines, when scripting an object's sequence, the sequence  
knows it belongs to a particular object only because it is nested in the Add Object to Scene macro.  
Finally, the order is important! Each scene runs in order, so, while many things can happen at once in a  
scene, the order of the scenes is determined by what order you add them.  
A cutscene with  
one scene, a single scene which  
camera, a single contains two  
object, and a single camera changes  
Adding a second  
action for that  
object.  
and two objects,  
each with a single  
action.  
Now, let's dig in to what each part of the cutscene really means.  
Cutscene Deﬁnition  
The Cutscene - Deﬁne Cutscene macro is the required header and footer for  
your cutscene - it encapsulates the whole thing and lets you set cutscene-wide  
parameters and also describe how to handle the end of the cutscene.  
Specify whether or not the cutscene is skippable (or only skippable on  
replay only [only on subsequent level playthroughs]).  
Specify whether or not you need to add player objects - if the cutscene  
does not contain the player characters, you should set this to 0.  
Specify the end camera transition properties and whether or not you  
want to transition back to the gameplay camera or a new camera (by ID)  
Finally, for the cutscene debugger to work completely, you should give  
me a set of actors that contain waypoints referenced by the current  
The Deﬁne Cutscene  
macro  
cutscene in its cameras, object init positions, and sequences. This has no eﬀect on the cutscene, but  
lets me give you information about what's going on.  
Scenes  
A scene is nothing more than a logical building block that lets you organize your cutscene. Within a scene,  
you may add camera and object scripting that will control what happens during that scene, and each scene  
speciﬁes a rule for when it should advance. There is no rule for how few or how many scenes you need for  
a cutscene (well, you need one), but scenes can help you easily do the following:  
Activate or deactivate objects as needed - one scene may include several NPCs that are not active in  
5 of 14  
01/11/2016 03:29 PM  
Cutscene System - tfbwiki  
http://tfb-wiki/w/index.php/Cutscene_System  
the following scene, so you can choose to only add them to the ﬁrst  
scene.  
Set up camera cuts.  
Set up a logical structure for your cutscene - for example, in the  
Using the Add Scene  
macro  
beginning of your cutscene, several NPCs may be walking to some  
waypoints. Afterward, the camera will be in a totally diﬀerent position  
while one of the NPCs speaks some dialogue. That sounds like a good  
case for multiple scenes: one for the walking, and one for the camera cut and dialogue.  
Scene Advance Condition  
The cutscene system knows to advance to the next scene based upon the *advance condition you specify,  
which is a macro specialization of Cutscene - Scene Advance Condition, that queries the current scene and  
states when it's ok to advance. The default advance condition, All Objects Done with Sequences, means  
what it sounds like: when all objects ﬁnish their sequences, the scene is complete. There is another built-in  
advance condition, All Camera Transitions Done, as well. If you have a very special case cutscene, you  
are allowed to write your own advance condition, but those two should cover almost every case.  
Cameras  
Camera commands fall into two categories: setting the camera [to look from  
somewhere, be an animator camera, etc.] and adjusting the camera. In each  
case, you will be able to specify common transition settings to control how  
long it takes to go from the camera's current position to the new one. Through  
the use of transition times and delays, you can create complex movements for  
the camera mid-scene.  
Setting a camera with the  
Cutscene Camera - Set  
Camera - Look From  
Camera Timing  
macro  
Imagine you had a scene where you wanted the camera to start somewhere,  
then transition to somewhere else over a period of time. You would place the  
camera with one of the Cutscene Camera - Set Camera macros, specifying a delay and transition time of  
0, followed by another usage of the same macro, but specifying a non-zero transition time. When the scene  
starts, the camera will pop to the ﬁrst location, then, over the transition time you speciﬁed, move to the  
second location.  
Continuing with that example, if you wanted another transition to occur after that transition time, you  
could use yet another Set Camera macro. The camera transitioning is entirely linear, meaning your  
camera controls will run in the order you put them, with each one waiting for the previous one to ﬁnish  
completely before it starts. This applies to delay, as well - the delay on a camera starts its timing when the  
previous camera is totally done with its transition.  
(TODO: CAMERA TIMING DIAGRAM)  
Camera controls should be placed at the top of the area within each scene. Cameras controls are tied to  
the scenes in which they reside. That is, if you have three scenes which each start oﬀ with a camera  
transition, the transition will only occur when that scene starts.  
Changing Cameras Mid-Sequence  
By default (placing your camera commands at the top of the space within the  
Add Scene macro), cameras run on their own timer and transition based on  
delays and transition time. However, if you so choose, you may place a  
set/adjust camera macro within an object's sequence. This will mean that,  
when the step above is complete, the camera command will occur. For  
Setting a camera  
example, you could have a character walk somewhere, turn, then speak  
mid-sequence  
dialogue. If you wanted a camera cut to happen right before he started  
6 of 14  
01/11/2016 03:29 PM  
Cutscene System - tfbwiki  
http://tfb-wiki/w/index.php/Cutscene_System  
speaking, you could place a Set Camera macro right before the dialogue step.  
Camera Types  
Look From  
The Look From camera lets you specify a single waypoint or object - the camera will position  
itself there and inherit its facing and tilt. If the object is moving, the camera will move with it.  
Look From/At  
The Look From/At camera lets you specify a look from waypoint/object as well as a look at  
waypoint/object - the camera will be positioned at the look from point and determine its  
facing/tilt based on the look at's heading/pitch from the camera. If the from/at is an object and is  
in motion, the camera will move with it.  
Animator Cam  
The Animator Cam lets you specify an animator camera animation actor and slot and behaves  
just like the Camera - Start Animator Cam macro.  
Adjust Camera  
So far, we've talked about using the Set Camera macros, but you may also  
move the camera to a relative location by using the Cutscene Camera -  
Adjust Camera macro. It will only function with a look from or look from/at  
camera, and it is very similar to the usage of Set Camera (it uses the same  
techniques for delay and transition time), except that, rather than specify a  
whole new camera, you're just allowed to oﬀset the current camera. You can  
use this to create subtle movements or eﬀects without having to generate new  
waypoints for positioning.  
Adjusting a camera with  
the Cutscene Camera -  
Adjust Camera macro  
Objects  
Objects are the actors in your cutscenes, both in the literal sense and the  
TFBTool sense. If you want an actor (be it an NPC, a wall, a bridge, etc.) to do  
things in your cutscene, you will want to make an object deﬁnition for it so the  
cutscene system understands how to interact with it. This can be done by  
utilizing the Cutscene - Deﬁne Object macro, specifying the *actor you wish  
to use, along with some other settings:  
Deﬁning a cutscene  
object with Cutscene -  
Deﬁne Object  
An *init technique and *shutdown technique, which are macros that  
will run to start up and shut down this object in the cutscene. The default  
settings are good for actors that are placed in the level, but you may wish to change those for use  
with, say, a templated actor that's meant to be cloned (you'd want to use Init Object - Clone and  
Shutdown Object - Remove). You may specify a null shutdown or startup technique, should you  
wish, and you may also write a custom one if you need it.  
You may optionally specify *init/update/shutdown actions, which are macros that will run when this  
object initializes, updates (while it's awake, this will run all the time), or shuts down, respectively.  
This could be something like attaching an object, adding particles, etc. Keep in mind that the actor  
you're specifying will still be allowed to run its script, if it has one, so you can manage visual elements  
there, if you want.  
You may also ﬂag the object to init only on scene init, meaning that it will not be activated until  
added to a scene - good for NPCs or things that will not always be on screen.  
When deﬁning an object, a CS - Object Def structure, *object def, is returned to you which you may hang  
7 of 14  
01/11/2016 03:29 PM  
Cutscene System - tfbwiki  
http://tfb-wiki/w/index.php/Cutscene_System  
on to via a local or global reference for later use in your cutscene(s).  
Player Objects  
Player object structures are returned as parameters in the Deﬁne Cutscene macro. You do not need to  
deﬁne the players, and you can access those objects via the returned parameters.  
Adding Objects to a Scene  
Deﬁning an object is just that - a deﬁnition for the actor. To actually make it a  
member of your cutscene and give it behaviors, you'll need to add it to a  
scene. To add an object to a scene, you utilize the aptly named Cutscene -  
Add Object to Scene macro within the scene where you want it added. This  
will make the object activate in that scene, along with some other, optional  
Adding an object to a  
scene with Cutscene -  
parameters:  
Add Object to Scene  
You may specify the *init position waypoint, in which case the actor  
will be placed there at the start of the scene.  
If you specify to shut down after scene, the object will shut down (running its *shutdown technique  
as implied above) after the scene is over. Unless the object will be used or visible in the following  
scene, this should be set to 1.  
Object Sequences  
Now we ﬁnally get to the fun part: an object is just an actor, and adding it to  
your scene merely lets you specify where he should be when the scene starts,  
but now we get to actually make him do something! An object's actions during  
a scene are controlled by what is called a sequence, which is a series of steps  
that run in order. When the last step completes, that object's sequence is  
complete. Each step is meant to be an encapsulated action: move here, play an  
animation, wait some time, speak some dialogue, etc.  
An example cutscene  
sequence using the  
pre-built Cutscene  
Sequence macros  
Before we continue, I do want to mention that it is very rare that cutscene  
scripters will actually be writing their own steps. The cutscene system  
provides many pre-deﬁned steps which cover most of the common, useful  
operations in cutscenes, which the cutscene writer will chain together for the  
desired eﬀect. That said, it may be useful to know what a step actually is,  
should you want to write your own or just so you can understand what's going  
on under the hood. I'd recommend skipping ahead to see some of the  
sequence steps already conceived and consider the below reading optional if  
you're not interested or feel overwhelmed.  
Adding a step to an object  
sequence with Cutscene  
- Add Object Sequence  
A step is speciﬁed with the Cutscene - Add Object Sequence macro, which  
is comprised of four basic components which you specify (macros that execute at speciﬁed times):  
The *step init action, which runs when the step starts.  
The *step update action, which runs while processing the step (every frame).  
The *step complete action, which runs when the step is completed.  
The *step advance condition, which determines when the step is done. This is the main control of  
an object sequence's ﬂow/timing - a step is active until the advance condition says it is time to  
proceed.  
When creating a sequence step, you are returned a structure in the form of the *return step def - this  
structure contains an untyped structure set, [step extra def data], which may be used to pass in additional  
information. For example, in the case of the Cutscene Sequence - Walk to Waypoint macro, I need keep  
information about what waypoint the object should walk to, what animation to play, and the speed  
8 of 14  
01/11/2016 03:29 PM  
Cutscene System - tfbwiki  
speciﬁed.  
http://tfb-wiki/w/index.php/Cutscene_System  
Sub-Sequences (Threading)  
While the general rule of sequences is that an object may only process one  
step at a time, each in order, the system allows for the authoring of sub-steps  
which spawn as threads of the parent step. In this case, the main step will  
start running, and then the sub-sequence will start running on its own. If the  
sub-sequence completes, but the parent step is not yet done, the sub-sequence  
has eﬀectively ended - the main sequence will not continue until the parent  
step is complete. If the parent step ﬁnishes (e.g. it's a play animation step and  
the animation is done), the sequence will then proceed, regardless of whether  
or not the sub-sequence is done - keep that in mind, since you may write a  
sub-sequence that won't be able to execute completely (e.g. if you put a 'delay  
for 5 seconds' and then a 'play sound' as sub-steps of a 'play animation' that  
only lasts 1 second, the sound will never play).  
Threaded sequences with  
Add Object Sequence's  
Layer 2 macro  
Similar to my remarks about cutscene writers not needing their own, custom steps, you can imagine it is  
even more unlikely that the cutscene author will need his own, custom sub-step. When utilizing the  
Cutscene Sequence macros, the notion of whether or not it's a sub-step is ﬁgured out for you by being  
entirely contextual: if you place a Delay for Time within a Play Animation, the delay for time has  
become a sub-step of the play animation.  
That said, if you wish to write your own, it is exactly the same as detailed above in the usage of Cutscene -  
Add Object Sequence, but you must use one of the alternately-named layer macros (e.g. Layer 2 or Layer  
3), since the TFBTool disallows calling a macro inside of itself. See the image to the right for an example of  
its usage.  
Object Specialization  
Though you may deﬁne objects just for use in a single cutscene (e.g. keeping a  
local structure reference to hang on to the object), some objects are probably  
going to see a lot of reuse in multiple cutscenes: Flynn, Cali, and maybe some  
other common NPCs. It makes sense to have just a single object deﬁnition for  
one of those characters, but sometimes you may need a specialization of the  
base object for use in a particular cutscene. By using the Cutscene - Deﬁne  
Object - Specialize for Cutscene macro, you are allowed to specify  
overrides and additional features which will only apply to that object in the  
current cutscene by using this nested within your Cutscene - Deﬁne  
Cutscene macro.  
Adding speciﬁc properties  
to an object for a single  
cutscene with Cutscene -  
Deﬁne Object -  
Specialize For Cutscene  
Pre-Built Features  
While the cutscene system provides a framework to do whatever you want, there are many canned  
operations that have been created to aid in the creation of cutscenes with minimal eﬀort and maximum  
reuse of assets/code. When talking about pre-built features, the distinction between them and the core  
cutscene system is that the features are all entirely optional (though you'll want to use them!), and, most  
importantly, they reside within the boundaries of the cutscene system. If the cutscene system is a skeleton,  
think of the pre-built features as known muscles like biceps and triceps to make the skeleton move - it's  
still the same skeleton, but the muscles are just known templates.  
I will not detail all the features here, just ones that beg for some explanation or introduction. You can  
peruse what's available by looking at the Cutscene Object and Cutscene Sequence macros.  
9 of 14  
01/11/2016 03:29 PM  
Cutscene System - tfbwiki  
http://tfb-wiki/w/index.php/Cutscene_System  
Object Features  
Object features are intended to be applied to cutscene objects (within the Cutscene -  
Deﬁne Object macro), and they exist in the macro submenu under Cutscene Object.  
Their usage is contextual - so just place them inside the Deﬁne Object macro, and you're  
good to go.  
Anim Map  
Deﬁning an  
Object, then  
adding an anim  
map and  
The animation map, added with the Cutscene Object - Deﬁne Anim Map macro,  
serves as a method of deﬁning common animations (and certain properties) for an  
object, so the system can reuse logic when dealing with NPCs. Additionally, the anim  
map is required for use with head turning, as it speciﬁes the object's head turning  
properties.  
speaker  
properties  
(NOTE): walk/walk-speed/run/run-speed/talk slots are not yet in use  
Speaker Properties  
Objects that will be speaking dialogue should use the Cutscene Object - Make  
Speaker macro to deﬁne their sprite and name strings.  
The list of  
available  
Cutscene  
Object macros  
as of 2-23-12  
Gestures  
Gestures are like named animation slots and are particularly useful when dealing with  
dialogue (see the Cutscene Sequence - Dialogue Gesture macro). There are a  
number of common, global gestures available, but objects may create their own  
gestures by creating a specialization of the Cutscene Object - Gesture macro by  
doing the following:  
An example  
gesture macro.  
Create a new specialization of the aforementioned macro.  
Set the anim index value to the desired animation slot.  
Though some macros will accept the gesture macro as an input, you may also just execute the macro if you  
want to extract the slot number somewhere.  
Sequence Features  
Sequence features are special wrappers for the Cutscene - Add Object  
Sequence macro, intended for modularly controlling an object's acting in a  
cutscene, and they exist in the macro submenu under Cutscene Sequence.  
Most of these are either self-explanatory or contain comments regarding how  
they can be used and chained together (walk to waypoint, play animation,  
etc.), but some will beneﬁt from some explanation.  
An example cutscene  
sequence using the  
pre-built Cutscene  
Sequence macros  
Dialogue  
Dialogue is fairly straightforward, in and of itself, but it has some neat features you can use, within it.  
When you start the dialogue step, the dialogue sound/UI process will start, and the step will not proceed  
10 of 14  
01/11/2016 03:29 PM  
Cutscene System - tfbwiki  
http://tfb-wiki/w/index.php/Cutscene_System  
until the dialogue is complete. However, as a sub-step of the dialogue, you may add the  
Cutscene Sequence - Delay for Dialogue Percent Remain, which does what it  
sounds like. If you want another object to delay for a certain moment in the dialogue,  
that object can use the same macro while specifying the speaking object and the ﬁrst  
dialogue string in the usage (so I know what to look up). For example, at a certain point  
in Flynn's dialogue, Cali could play a reactive animation.  
Additionally, the Dialogue macro will inheret information about the speaker's  
name/sprite based on the usage of the Cutscene Object - Make Speaker macro.  
The list of  
available  
Cutscene  
Sequence  
macros as of  
2-23-12  
Dialogue Gesturing  
You may add animations to a character while s/he speaks dialogue by nesting  
the Cutscene Sequence - Dialogue Gesture macro. Just specify an at  
dialogue percent remain at which point the gesture animation should occur,  
and pick the *gesture to play. See here for information on setting up gestures.  
Dialogue with two  
gestures.  
Head Turning  
Objects utilizing actors which have been set up with an animation map and the appropriate head turning  
control animations can utilize the Cutscene Sequence - Look at Object step to look at another object. By  
default, the object will turn its head using the speed speciﬁed in the animation map, and sequence will not  
delay while the head turn takes place. If you wish, you may cause the step to wait for the head turn to  
complete with the wait for turn complete to proceed parameter. You can also use a ﬁxed turn time (for  
dramatic eﬀect!) with the force turn time, which will utilize that time for the current turn action. If the  
character needs to pivot because the head turn is more than 90 degrees, then the ﬁxed turn time will be  
ignored.  
Triggering Level Objects  
Sometimes, there will be a game mechanic (e.g. a gate) that a designer has set  
up which runs its own script and has its own behaviors which control how it  
works, but you need to use it in a cutscene. The cutscene-side method for this  
lies in the Cutscene Sequence - Trigger Level Object macro, but there is a  
bit of setup on the level object/mechanic side:  
In the level object, make a new macro specialization of Cutscene  
Sequence - Trigger Level Object - Logic Macro. The interface will  
provide a *triggering object parameter, which will point to the level  
object being triggered by the cutscene. You'll want to activate the actor's  
mechanic by whatever means make sense - for example, the gate could  
Setting up a gate to be  
triggered by a cutscene  
11 of 14  
01/11/2016 03:29 PM  
Cutscene System - tfbwiki  
http://tfb-wiki/w/index.php/Cutscene_System  
have a variable named force drop, and this macro could ﬁnd that variable and set it to 1 to make the  
gate drop.  
In the level object's startup, use the Cutscene Sequence - Trigger Level Object - Deﬁne Trigger  
Method macro, and deﬁne the macro you just wrote for the *init trigger parameter. This actually ties  
that macro to this actor.  
In the level object's shutdown, use the Clear Trigger Method specialization of the aforementioned  
Cutscene Sequence - Trigger Level Object - Deﬁne Trigger Method macro to clean up when this  
actor shuts down.  
In the cutscene, when you are ready to trigger the object, use the Cutscene Sequence - Trigger  
Level Object macro, and the object you just set up should respond.  
(optional) If the object has an animation to its being triggered - continuing with the gate example,  
there could be an animation of the gate dropping - then the object may also create a Cutscene  
Sequence - Trigger Level Object - Advance Condition specialization and specify it in the Deﬁne  
Trigger Method macro. When speciﬁed, the step will not advance until this macro sets the advance  
now parameter - for example, it could wait until the gate's animation is done or a variable reads a  
certain number.  
Why all this eﬀort just to open a gate? The idea is that the cutscene scripter doesn't necessarily know the  
required script to open gates, doors, etc., and this creates a common interface for both level/mechanic  
designer and cutscene scripter. Cinematics, which will be scripts all over the place, won't need to be  
updated if the triggering methods change or evolve - e.g. if the ﬁnd variable line for a gate changes, only  
the gate's own method macro needs to be updated, not every cutscene triggering a gate.  
Notes on Cutscene Skipping  
Cutscenes written with the cutscene system are able to skip instantly - that is, unlike Sky1 cutscenes, I can  
cause the cutscene to complete in a single frame without needing to run the game really fast. As  
mentioned before, all you are writing is a cutscene narrative, and then, much later, the system executes all  
the commands you give it. Each step has an init, update, and complete action, so when I skip the cutscene,  
I just blow through every step, running each init and complete action. With smart design of those action  
macros (the macros even pass in a parameter for whether or not we're skipping!), any activity can be made  
'skippable.'  
When utilizing the pre-built cutscene sequence macros, I make sure to support skipping in all cases. For  
example, the Walk Obj to Location macro has some logic in the complete action that says: "If we're  
skipping, warp the object to the target location." Suddenly, a walk sequence which would take 5 seconds of  
normal time can be forced to complete immediately.  
Animator Cutscenes  
Streaming Cutscenes  
Streaming cutscenes allow you to utilize a stream to load in a cutscene for playing, instead of having the  
cutscene available within the current level. While the cutscene, itself, is implemented via the same  
methods, above, there is a little bit of setup in the main level to create a header so you can tell the system  
to load in and play your streaming cutscene.  
Setup  
The two pieces you need for a streaming cutscene are a string which is accessible by the main level and a  
12 of 14  
01/11/2016 03:29 PM  
Cutscene System - tfbwiki  
http://tfb-wiki/w/index.php/Cutscene_System  
streaming level which is built (likely included in the level) that has your cutscene. Let's say you have a  
cutscene for the level, Level_300, called Level_300_IGC_Intro.lvl which you want to stream. Follow these  
steps:  
Make an actor which is 0,0 activation/deactivation and untemplated, so it activates immediately, and  
give it a new script. To that script's startup, add a usage of the macro Cutscene - Streaming -  
Streamed Cutscene Deﬁne and specify the deﬁne macro for the cutscene. This will tell the cutscene  
system how to play your cutscene once the stream is loaded.  
Mark the level to load as a streaming character by checking the Streamed box and selecting the  
Character radio button.  
Now you have a cutscene stream set up and ready to go. You just need to include it in the main level, so it  
builds, and tell the main level about how to load and play it.  
Open the main level and add a new string to be the ﬁlename of your stream, setting the text to be the  
stream's ﬁle (Level_300_IGC_Intro in the above example). Make sure to mark the do not translate  
button since it's a constant ﬁlename.  
Somewhere in the main level, like the level deﬁnition, use the macro Cutscene - Streaming - Deﬁne  
Cutscene Header and specify the string you just created.  
When you want to play the streaming cutscene, use the Cutscene - Streaming - Play Streaming  
Cutscene macro and specify the same string.  
The cutscene will load in and play as soon as it's loaded, by default, but you may also minimize  
perceived load time for the player by using this macro when you are about to need to play it,  
such as when the player is approaching an NPC, and setting the play immediately parameter to  
0. When you are actually ready to play the cutscene, use the macro again and set the parameter  
to 1.  
File Structure Recommendations  
Although the above details the simplest process for streaming cutscenes, I'd recommend a particular ﬁle  
structure to make iterating upon and packaging the cutscenes as straightforward as possible. For each  
streaming cutscene, make a non-streamed level which:  
Includes and packs in the cutscene stream lvl.  
Has the string to load the stream lvl.  
Has an actor that starts up immediately (0/0) and runs the Deﬁne Cutscene Header macro to set  
things up for the designers, then removes itself.  
Then tell designers to include that non-streamed lvl. This isolates the set up of the streaming cutscene to a  
single package, and all designers need to do is use the Cutscene - Streaming - Play Streaming  
Cutscene macro without needing to know much about the set up. It also makes the streaming cutscene  
'package' modular and easy to play from any level which wishes to include it.  
The Cutscene Debugger  
While a cutscene is running, you may access the cutscene debugger UI by pressing up on the dpad.  
Though the debugger can be used for debugging, the primary use of it is to help the cutscene scripter (or  
another asset creator like an animator or sound designer) iterate upon his cutscene. The debugger  
provides the following features:  
Play/pause cutscene  
Previous/next scene  
Slow/speed cutscene playback  
Loop a single scene  
Loop the whole cutscene  
Restart the cutscene (useful when live updating a cutscene script)  
Toggle the display of object sequence information (what step the object is in, and what that step is  
doing)  
Toggle the display of camera information (what camera is in use, how much time until the next  
13 of 14  
01/11/2016 03:29 PM  
Cutscene System - tfbwiki  
camera)  
http://tfb-wiki/w/index.php/Cutscene_System  
Because of the nature of cameras, skipping and  
looping scenes may create some strange results  
(since we may not be coming from the usual camera  
when skipping forward).
